APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses abstractive sentence summarization—a core NLP task—using a novel selective encoding mechanism and standard sequence-to-sequence architectures, which is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) against appropriate strong baselines (s2s+att, ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s) using established metrics (ROUGE), and reports hyperparameters and thorough related work, indicating it targets the right audience and uses expected methods/evaluation. The problem formulation and experimental setup match community standards, and the writing quality and positioning appear suitable for a major conference. | B(5): This paper addresses abstractive sentence summarization with a novel selective gate module and standard seq2seq+attention architectures, which squarely fits the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on canonical datasets (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) against strong baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT) using standard ROUGE metrics, matching community expectations for methodology and evaluation. The problem formulation, audience, and positioning (related work coverage, reported hyperparameters) are appropriate for a high-tier conference.

The problem formulation and experimental setup match community standards, and the writing quality and positioning appear suitable for a major conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses abstractive sentence summarization with a novel selective gate module and standard seq2seq+attention architectures, which squarely fits the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on canonical datasets (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) against strong baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT) using standard ROUGE metrics, matching community expectations for methodology and evaluation. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of abstractive sentence summarization and introduces a simple, general selective gate mechanism (BiGRU encoder + selective gate + attention GRU decoder) to produce tailored sentence representations that reduce the decoder’s burden. It demonstrably outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR), which makes the approach likely to be adopted and extended; the selective encoding idea and the saliency-based analysis also open clear follow-up directions in model design and interpretability. | B(3): The paper addresses an important subproblem in NLP—abstractive sentence summarization—by proposing a selective encoding model (BiGRU encoder + selective gate + attention GRU decoder) that reportedly outperforms prior baselines on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) using ROUGE metrics. The selective gate idea, which tailors per-word representations and reduces decoder burden, is a practical architectural contribution that researchers are likely to build on for similar sequence-to-sequence tasks. However, the contribution is an incremental architectural improvement limited to sentence-level summarization rather than a broad paradigm shift, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of abstractive sentence summarization and introduces a simple, general selective gate mechanism (BiGRU encoder + selective gate + attention GRU decoder) to produce tailored sentence representations that reduce the decoder’s burden. It demonstrably outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR), which makes the approach likely to be adopted and extended; the selective encoding idea and the saliency-based analysis also open clear follow-up directions in model design and interpretability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important subproblem in NLP—abstractive sentence summarization—by proposing a selective encoding model (BiGRU encoder + selective gate + attention GRU decoder) that reportedly outperforms prior baselines on standard benchmarks (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) using ROUGE metrics. The selective gate idea, which tailors per-word representations and reduces decoder burden, is a practical architectural contribution that researchers are likely to build on for similar sequence-to-sequence tasks. However, the contribution is an incremental architectural improvement limited to sentence-level summarization rather than a broad paradigm shift, so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are fairly comprehensive: the model is evaluated against strong baselines on English Gigaword, DUC 2004 and MSR with ROUGE metrics, and architecture/hyperparameter details are reported. However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., removing the selective gate or varying its size), although a saliency/first-derivative analysis is presented. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations (small/noisy test sets, visualization difficulty) are discussed. Code availability is not mentioned, so full reproducibility may still have minor gaps despite reported hyperparameters. | B(4): The paper evaluates the model on three standard datasets (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) and compares against multiple strong baselines using ROUGE metrics, which supports the main performance claims. It reports hyperparameters and provides a qualitative analysis of the selective gate via saliency heat maps, but no explicit ablation or sensitivity study isolating the selective gate’s contribution is mentioned. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations (small DUC training set, noisy Gigaword test lines, visualization difficulty) are acknowledged. Code release is not mentioned, so reproducibility is supported by hyperparameter reporting but could be stronger.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are fairly comprehensive: the model is evaluated against strong baselines on English Gigaword, DUC 2004 and MSR with ROUGE metrics, and architecture/hyperparameter details are reported. However, the summary does not indicate explicit ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., removing the selective gate or varying its size), although a saliency/first-derivative analysis is presented. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations (small/noisy test sets, visualization difficulty) are discussed. Code availability is not mentioned, so full reproducibility may still have minor gaps despite reported hyperparameters.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates the model on three standard datasets (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) and compares against multiple strong baselines using ROUGE metrics, which supports the main performance claims. It reports hyperparameters and provides a qualitative analysis of the selective gate via saliency heat maps, but no explicit ablation or sensitivity study isolating the selective gate’s contribution is mentioned. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations (small DUC training set, noisy Gigaword test lines, visualization difficulty) are acknowledged. Code release is not mentioned, so reproducibility is supported by hyperparameter reporting but could be stronger.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper proposes a selective encoding mechanism (SEASS) that novelly applies a per-token selective gate sGatei = sigmoid(Ws hi + Us s + b) to produce tailored encoder representations h'i = hi * sGatei before attention decoding. This is a clear architectural contribution that differentiates it from standard BiGRU+attention baselines and prior summarization models listed. However, the idea is an incremental extension of existing sequence-to-sequence and attention techniques (a gating/feature-selection variant) rather than a fundamentally new problem formulation or paradigm, so its novelty is moderate. | B(4): The paper introduces a concrete new component — a selective gate network that computes per-word gate vectors sGatei = sigmoid(Ws hi + Us s + b) to produce tailored encoder representations h'i = hi * sGatei — which is a clear architectural novelty beyond standard BiGRU+attention seq2seq models. The selective encoding perspective (conditioning word-level representations on a sentence vector before decoding) is a non-trivial extension and is presented as distinct from prior baselines (s2s+att, ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s). While sentence summarization is a well-studied problem, the specific selective gating mechanism and its analysis (saliency visualization) would likely inform experts and represents notable novelty rather than merely combining known techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a selective encoding mechanism (SEASS) that novelly applies a per-token selective gate sGatei = sigmoid(Ws hi + Us s + b) to produce tailored encoder representations h'i = hi * sGatei before attention decoding. This is a clear architectural contribution that differentiates it from standard BiGRU+attention baselines and prior summarization models listed. However, the idea is an incremental extension of existing sequence-to-sequence and attention techniques (a gating/feature-selection variant) rather than a fundamentally new problem formulation or paradigm, so its novelty is moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a concrete new component — a selective gate network that computes per-word gate vectors sGatei = sigmoid(Ws hi + Us s + b) to produce tailored encoder representations h'i = hi * sGatei — which is a clear architectural novelty beyond standard BiGRU+attention seq2seq models. The selective encoding perspective (conditioning word-level representations on a sentence vector before decoding) is a non-trivial extension and is presented as distinct from prior baselines (s2s+att, ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s). While sentence summarization is a well-studied problem, the specific selective gating mechanism and its analysis (saliency visualization) would likely inform experts and represents notable novelty rather than merely combining known techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear main claims (the selective gate architecture and improved abstractive summarization) and supports them with experiments against appropriate baselines (s2s+att, ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, etc.) on standard test sets (Gigaword, DUC2004, MSR) using ROUGE metrics. The model formulation (BiGRU encoder, sigmoid gating, attention-equipped GRU decoder) is simple and mathematically sound, and hyperparameters are reported. A minor concern is that the summary does not mention statistical significance testing of improvements and the selective-gate analysis relies on an indirect first-derivative saliency approximation, which are weaknesses but do not undermine the main experimental support. Overall, the technical claims are well supported with only minor methodological omissions. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental results showing SEASS outperforms listed baselines (s2s+att, ABS variants, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT) on English Gigaword, DUC 2004 and MSR using ROUGE metrics. The selective gate formulation (sGate_i = sigmoid(W_s h_i + U_s s + b), h'_i = h_i * sGate_i) is mathematically straightforward and complete for the proposed mechanism. The experimental methodology is generally sound—using standard datasets and strong baselines and reporting hyperparameters—but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and the interpretability claim relies on an acknowledged approximation (first-derivative saliency). These are minor gaps that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear main claims (the selective gate architecture and improved abstractive summarization) and supports them with experiments against appropriate baselines (s2s+att, ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, etc.) on standard test sets (Gigaword, DUC2004, MSR) using ROUGE metrics. The model formulation (BiGRU encoder, sigmoid gating, attention-equipped GRU decoder) is simple and mathematically sound, and hyperparameters are reported. A minor concern is that the summary does not mention statistical significance testing of improvements and the selective-gate analysis relies on an indirect first-derivative saliency approximation, which are weaknesses but do not undermine the main experimental support. Overall, the technical claims are well supported with only minor methodological omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental results showing SEASS outperforms listed baselines (s2s+att, ABS variants, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT) on English Gigaword, DUC 2004 and MSR using ROUGE metrics. The selective gate formulation (sGate_i = sigmoid(W_s h_i + U_s s + b), h'_i = h_i * sGate_i) is mathematically straightforward and complete for the proposed mechanism. The experimental methodology is generally sound—using standard datasets and strong baselines and reporting hyperparameters—but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release, and the interpretability claim relies on an acknowledged approximation (first-derivative saliency). These are minor gaps that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction to method to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (selective gate network and SEASS architecture) are stated early. The notation is consistently presented (e.g., hi, s, sGatei and h'i) and experimental details, baselines, datasets, and hyperparameters are reported, which aids clarity. One minor presentation issue is that the selective gate is high-dimensional and hard to visualize directly, requiring an approximate first-derivative saliency heat map, which slightly reduces interpretability of the presented analyses. Overall the writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough. | B(4): The paper appears well organized with a clear progression from model description (BiGRU encoder, selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder) to experiments on Gigaword, DUC2004 and MSR—so the logical flow is intact. The main contributions (the selective gate idea and its role in reducing decoder burden) are stated early, and the notation for hi, s, and sGatei is given and consistent. Figures/tables and examples (e.g., first-derivative saliency heat maps, baseline comparisons, ROUGE results, and reported hyperparameters) are informative, but the authors note a presentational limitation: the high-dimensional gate outputs are hard to visualize and require approximation. Also, minor dataset-cleaning issues (invalid lines in Gigaword and DUC2004 being too small for training) are acknowledged and could be more explicitly documented.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from introduction to method to experiments and conclusions, and the main contributions (selective gate network and SEASS architecture) are stated early. The notation is consistently presented (e.g., hi, s, sGatei and h'i) and experimental details, baselines, datasets, and hyperparameters are reported, which aids clarity. One minor presentation issue is that the selective gate is high-dimensional and hard to visualize directly, requiring an approximate first-derivative saliency heat map, which slightly reduces interpretability of the presented analyses. Overall the writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well organized with a clear progression from model description (BiGRU encoder, selective gate, attention-equipped GRU decoder) to experiments on Gigaword, DUC2004 and MSR—so the logical flow is intact. The main contributions (the selective gate idea and its role in reducing decoder burden) are stated early, and the notation for hi, s, and sGatei is given and consistent. Figures/tables and examples (e.g., first-derivative saliency heat maps, baseline comparisons, ROUGE results, and reported hyperparameters) are informative, but the authors note a presentational limitation: the high-dimensional gate outputs are hard to visualize and require approximation. Also, minor dataset-cleaning issues (invalid lines in Gigaword and DUC2004 being too small for training) are acknowledged and could be more explicitly documented.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (s2s+att, ABS / ABS+, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT) on standard datasets (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR) using common ROUGE metrics, and reports hyperparameters. Related work coverage is stated as thorough and the paper analyzes differences (selective gate mechanism and saliency visualizations) rather than merely reporting numbers. It is not fully clear from the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so I mark a minor omission rather than a major flaw. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (s2s+att, ABS/ABS+, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT) on standard test sets (English Gigaword, DUC2004, MSR) using ROUGE-1/2/L, so comparisons appear fair and on common conditions. The authors analyze the proposed selective gate (first-derivative saliency heat maps) and report hyperparameters, and claim outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. However, the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better and the analysis of differences beyond the saliency visualization seems limited, so there are minor omissions rather than major flaws.

Related work coverage is stated as thorough and the paper analyzes differences (selective gate mechanism and saliency visualizations) rather than merely reporting numbers. It is not fully clear from the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so I mark a minor omission rather than a major flaw.

The authors analyze the proposed selective gate (first-derivative saliency heat maps) and report hyperparameters, and claim outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. main_claims: Introduce a selective encoding model (SEASS) that uses a selective gate network to produce tailored sentence representations for abstractive sentence summarization.

The selective encoding model (BiGRU encoder + selective gate + attention-equipped GRU decoder) yields better performance and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on English Gigaword, DUC 2004 and MSR test sets.
The selective gate effectively determines the importance of each input word (reducing decoder burden) and this can be analyzed via a first-derivative saliency heat map.
_summary.methodology: A bidirectional GRU encoder produces per-word vectors hi and a sentence vector s; a selective gate network computes gate vectors sGatei = sigmoid(Ws hi + Us s + b) and produces tailored representations h'i = hi * sGatei, which are fed to an attention-equipped GRU decoder to generate abstractive summaries.
datasets: MSR-ATC (MSR abstractive dataset by Toutanova et al., 2016)
limitations: DUC 2004 dataset is too small to train a neural network model (stated as a reason for using it only as a test set).
The English Gigaword test set contains some invalid lines (e.g., empty titles or input sentences with only one word).
The selective gate output is high-dimensional and hard to visualize directly (requires approximation via first-derivative saliency).
